name: Sophia Amoruso
title: Sophia Amoruso: The #Girlboss Who Sold Vintage and a Whole Vibe
dek: From dumpster-diving and an eBay store to a fashion juggernaut, a bankruptcy, a bestseller, and a comeback — she wrote the messy, magnetic playbook of the internet-era founder.
body_html:
Before "side hustle" was a LinkedIn cliché, Sophia Amoruso was hawking thrifted leather jackets out of an eBay store called Nasty Gal Vintage. No business degree. No investors. No master plan. Just a sharp eye, a hustler's instinct, and a knack for making secondhand clothes feel like contraband you couldn't live without. What she built next turned her into one of the defining founder-characters of the social-media generation.

The Come-Up: From Hitchhiking to Hashtags

Amoruso's origin story is gleefully un-corporate. She's written about a teenage-and-twenties era of odd jobs, hitchhiking, and admitted shoplifting before she stumbled into reselling vintage online around 2006. The genius move? She didn't just sell the clothes — she styled and shot them herself, photographing pieces on models in a way that made a $20 jacket look like a magazine spread.

She studied her buyers obsessively, learning what made people click "buy now" in the early eBay-and-MySpace ecosystem. When eBay's rules cramped her style, she spun the whole thing into a standalone site. Nasty Gal was born — and it grew like a rumor.

The Creativity: Selling a Personality, Not Just Product

Here's what Amoruso understood years before the influencer economy made it obvious: she wasn't selling clothes, she was selling a character you wanted to be. The Nasty Gal girl was bold, a little dangerous, effortlessly cool. The brand's voice on social media was flirty and irreverent. The customer photos, the community, the attitude — it all turned shoppers into a tribe.

She rode social media as a free, turbocharged marketing engine, building a following that felt less like a customer list and more like a fan club. Then in 2014 she dropped #GIRLBOSS, a memoir-slash-manifesto that became a cultural lightning rod, spawning a hit phrase, a Netflix series, and a whole genre of "I did it my way" entrepreneurship.

She didn't market a store. She bottled an identity and sold it by the thousands.

The Crash, the Comeback, and Class

The fairy tale got real. Nasty Gal scaled into a company reportedly valued in the hundreds of millions at its peak, but rapid growth, retail headwinds, and operational stumbles caught up with it. The company filed for bankruptcy in 2016, and the brand was eventually sold off — a humbling, very public faceplant that would've ended most founders' stories.

Amoruso did the un-girlboss thing and got back up. She channeled the lessons into Business Class and other ventures aimed at teaching the next wave of founders how to build — and crucially, how to survive the parts the highlight reel skips. She's been refreshingly honest that the buzzword she helped popularize curdled, and she's spent her second act peeling back the gloss to talk about the real, unglamorous grind.

Her exact net worth is a moving target, swinging with each venture, but the through-line is undeniable: she turned taste, audacity, and a phone camera into a multimillion-dollar brand once, and she's spent the years since proving the first time wasn't luck.

The kicker: Sophia Amoruso didn't just sell vintage — she sold the idea that the weird kid with no resume could run the whole show.
